title: Lemon shortbread
subtitle: Crisp, buttery Scottish-style shortbread with lemon zest
excerpt: A classic Scottish shortbread using the 3:2:1 ratio of flour to butter to sugar, with lemon zest worked into the dough and a small amount of fine semolina for a slight sandy texture. The bake is slow at a low temperature to keep the shortbread pale and prevent the butter from scorching. The whole batch takes about 45 minutes.
sourceNotes: Shortbread is documented in Scotland from the sixteenth century, most famously associated with Mary Queen of Scots. The classic 3:2:1 flour-butter-sugar ratio appears in F. Marian McNeill's 'The Scots Kitchen' (1929) and in later editions of Mrs Beeton. Adding a small amount of semolina or ground rice for a sandy crumb is a Scottish tradition that predates the flour-only version.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze in a tin for up to 3 months. Thaw at room temperature for 1 hour.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps for 2 weeks at room temperature in an airtight tin. The shortbread continues to crisp and improve after the first day.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; slow bake to keep the shortbread pale

glossaryTerms.definition: The classic formula of 3 parts flour to 2 parts butter to 1 part sugar by weight. This ratio gives shortbread its distinctive snap, buttery flavour and dry, crumbly texture. Reducing the butter makes a harder, less rich biscuit; increasing it makes the dough too soft to handle. Replacing a small portion of the flour with fine semolina or ground rice adds a barely perceptible grittiness that gives each bite a slight crunch and helps the shortbread brown evenly without turning golden.

content.text: with a small swap of semolina for some of the flour, which gives the traditional Scottish sandy texture. Work the dough as little as possible once the flour goes in: overworking develops gluten and makes the shortbread tough rather than crumbly.

Use caster sugar rather than icing sugar for a slightly more grainy texture and better flavour. Press the lemon zest into the sugar with your fingers before creaming; this breaks the zest cells and releases the oils directly into the sugar.

Rub the lemon zest into the 75 g caster sugar with your fingers. Beat the 150 g softened butter with the lemon sugar and a pinch of salt until pale and creamy, about 2 minutes. Stir in the 200 g flour and 25 g semolina until the mixture comes together into a soft dough. Do not knead.

Heat the oven to 160 degrees C fan. Press or roll the dough out to 8 mm thick on a lightly floured surface. Cut into fingers (about 8 x 2.5 cm) or rounds with a cutter. Place on a lined baking tray. Prick each piece several times with a fork. Bake for 22 to 28 minutes until firm, pale cream in colour with the faintest blush of gold at the very edges. The shortbread should not brown. Sprinkle with caster sugar while still warm, then cool completely on a rack before moving.

items.problem: Shortbread is tough and hard rather than crumbly
items.solution: The dough was overworked once the flour was added, developing gluten. Mix only until the dough just comes together. If the dough was also rolled more than once, rerolling also develops the gluten.

items.problem: Shortbread browned before it was fully baked
items.solution: The oven temperature was too high. Shortbread must be baked slowly at 160 degrees C or below to stay pale. If your oven runs hot, reduce to 150 degrees C and add 5 minutes to the baking time.
Rub the zest into the sugar before creaming: this releases the aromatic oils immediately into the fat. Use unwaxed lemons and zest them finely; the zest must come from both lemons, not just one.
